coredata.dc:title: Influence of intentional phosphorus doping on Brassica juncea-derived unintentionally N, Ca-doped carbon quantum dots@bioplastic composites for applications in LED and UV-tube down-conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Varun Dutt
Kansay, Vishal
Bhatia, Anita
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based composite as a luminescent down-conversion materials are gaining importance nowadays because of many favorable factors including stable fluorescence, ease of functionalization, tuning of emission in visible range etc. In this work, we report an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract. The as-synthesized CQDs were unintentionally doped with nitrogen and calcium without the inclusion of any doping element precursors. However, phosphorus was intentionally doped to improve the luminescent characteristics of as-synthesized CQDs. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical properties of P-doped CQDs were examined. The quasi-circular or polygonal-shaped CQDs, with an average diameter of 3.4 nm, emerged to produce yellow-greenish fluorescence upon UV stimulation, demonstrate outstanding photostability, and had a quantum yield of up to 18.2%. Furthermore, the ecologically friendly and biodegradable bioplastic is attractive as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs. Hence, this work revealed the effect of phosphorus doping on solid-state fluorescence in the down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes by producing a composite constructed through inserting CQDs into the solid-state matrix of corn-starch-based bioplastic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Bioplastic
LED
UV tubes

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green-synthesis of carbon quantum dots derived from Brassica juncea
Fabrication of optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
Optical properties of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca, P-doped CQDs
Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Brassica juncea derived N,Ca,P-doped CQDs
Applications of Brassica juncea derived N,Ca,P-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite for down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow diagram for a cost-effective and environmentally sustainable synthesis of intentional phosphorus doping using Brassica juncea flower extract as a carbon source via hydrothermal method.

(a)–(c) HRTEM images, d-spacing from HR-TEM images, (d) Particle size distribution histogram fitted with a Lorentzian model, (e) SAED patterns, and (f) EDX profile of intentionally P-doped CQDs derived from Brassica juncea flower extract.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of intentionally P-doped CQDs. High resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s s, (d) O 1s, (e) Ca 2p and (f) P 2p spectrum.
(a) X-ray diffraction pattern and (b) FT-IR spectra of Brassica juncea flower extract, as-synthesized and intentionally P- doped CQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding (b) cut-off wavelengths or threshold absorption wavelengths of CQDs synthesized with and without intentional phosphorus doping.

(a) A comparison of FL spectra obtained from CQDs synthesized with and without intentional phosphorus doping. (b) Deconvolution of the FL spectra and (c) digital photographs of aqueous suspended CQDs emitting bright and vivid green fluorescence upon UV excitation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) A comparison of normalized FL spectra obtained from various pH dependent intentionally P-doped CQDs. (b)–(e) corresponding deconvoluted FL spectra.
(a) Excitation dependent FL spectra of intentionally P-doped CQDs. (b) The corresponding contour plot of excitation vs. emission wavelength.
Proposed FL mechanisms for Brassica juncea derived CQDs.
Digital photographs of fabricated thin film on glass slide using CQDs@bioplastic composite that was exposed to white and UV light.

Digital photographs of fabricated down-conversion UV-LED chip made of pH-sensitive intentionally P-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite. The concerned electroluminescent spectrum and CIE chromaticity coordinates according to CIE 1931 are also shown.

Digital photographs of commercially available UVC tube (254 nm, 11 W) (a) during OFF and ON condition, (b) wrapped with bioplastic film only, wrapped with (c) unintentionally N,Ca-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite and (d) intentionally P-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite along with electroluminescent and CIE spectrum.

head.ce:title: Influence of intentional phosphorus doping on Brassica juncea-derived unintentionally N, Ca-doped carbon quantum dots@bioplastic composites for applications in LED and UV-tube down-conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, MMEC, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, MMEC, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana. Ambala, 133207, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, DIT University, Uttarakhand, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, DIT University, Uttarakhand, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based composite as a luminescent down-conversion materials are gaining importance nowadays because of many favorable factors including stable fluorescence, ease of functionalization, tuning of emission in visible range etc. In this work, we report an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract. The as-synthesized CQDs were unintentionally doped with nitrogen and calcium without the inclusion of any doping element precursors. However, phosphorus was intentionally doped to improve the luminescent characteristics of as-synthesized CQDs. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical properties of P-doped CQDs were examined. The quasi-circular or polygonal-shaped CQDs, with an average diameter of 3.4 nm, emerged to produce yellow-greenish fluorescence upon UV stimulation, demonstrate outstanding photostability, and had a quantum yield of up to 18.2%. Furthermore, the ecologically friendly and biodegradable bioplastic is attractive as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs. Hence, this work revealed the effect of phosphorus doping on solid-state fluorescence in the down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes by producing a composite constructed through inserting CQDs into the solid-state matrix of corn-starch-based bioplastic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The most energy-efficient option for global lighting today is LED lighting. Development of wavelength down-conversion is an essential and economic strategy in LED lighting technology in which high-energy or shorter wavelength light from near-UV or blue emissive LEDs is absorbed by the down conversion materials and then re-emitted as lower energy or shorter wavelength visible light. The LED lighting industry is anticipated to be worth USD 124.7 billion by 2027, with a compound annual growth rate of 10.6% [1]. Moreover, technological advances and growing environmental concerns are accelerating the expansion of the LED lighting industry. LED lighting is now commonplace in homes, hospitals, schools, and other commercial and industrial sectors. Due to their low energy consumption, high light efficiency, long lifespan, and lack of radiation, LEDs are widely used in lighting, backlighting, and display [2–6]. Therefore, it is essential to develop LED technology that is more affordable and environmentally friendly. In terms of down-conversion luminous materials, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), inorganic or rare-earth phosphor materials, and organic or organometallic dyes have all received considerable attention to date [7–12].

In this regards, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining importance as a cost-effective and ecologically sustainable luminous material. CQDs have demonstrated outstanding properties such as being environmentally friendly, easy to synthesize, non-bleaching, high biocompatibility, high photostability, multi-photon excitation capacity, ease of functionalization, excellent solubility in aqueous solution etc. Since their unexpected discovery in early 2004 [13,14]. Excitation-dependent fluorescence is common in CQDs, owing to surface states or defects that cause fluorescence. Researchers are doping CQDs with a number of elements, including nitrogen, boron, Sulphur, phosphorus, and others, to obtain an excitation-dependent fluorescence nature via surface states and defects [15–23].

However, in the search for simple, cost-effective, and ecologically sustainable precursors and synthesis processes, hydrothermal-based green-synthesis of CQDs generated from natural biomaterials is particularly appealing and has the potential for large-scale manufacturing [8]. In addition, biomaterials extract contains a diverse range of plant metabolites, including alkaloids, minerals, vitamins, glycosides, phenolics, terpenoids, and flavonoids, which may be effective for doping various elements during CQD synthesis without the need for additional doping element precursors [38]. On the other hand, due to the negative environmental consequences of synthetic plastics, the development of biodegradable polymers for industrial and commercial applications is crucial today. This ecologically sustainable and biodegradable bioplastic would be a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs.

Therefore, in this study we report an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract. The as-synthesized CQDs were unintentionally doped with nitrogen and calcium without the inclusion of any doping element precursors. However, phosphorus was intentionally doped to improve the luminescent characteristics of as-synthesized CQDs [24]. The influence of phosphorus doping on solid-state fluorescence in down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes have been demonstrated by constructing a composite made by embedding CQDs into the solid-state matrix of corn-starch-based bioplastic.

Mustard flower (scientific name–Brassica juncea, botanical family– Brassicaceae) as a precursor for CQDs was taken from the nearby agricultural field of the University campus (GPS locations: 30°25’ N, 77° 04′E). In this experiment, only analytical-grade compounds were employed. Without any further purification, all substances were utilized exactly as they were received. The Whatman filter paper (grade1), 220 nm syringe filter made of polytetrafluoroethylene membrane, acetone, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, polyethylene glycol and phosphoric acid were among the additional supplies bought from a commercial supplier. Deionized water was double distilled using a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm−1, Millipore, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green-synthesis of carbon quantum dots derived from Brassica juncea
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were produced from Brassica juncea flower extracts using a quick and simple hydrothermal-based one-step green synthesis technique. To begin, the Brassica juncea flower was crushed in a domestic mixer grinder to extract the juice, which was then filtered using Whatmann filter paper. Following that, two distinct experiments were carried out. First, the Brassica juncea flower extract was put alone (herein denoted as sample #1) in a 50 ml Teflon-liner, and then the same solution was combined with phosphoric acid (9:1 v/v ratio) to intentionally dope with phosphorus (herein denoted as sample #2) before being sonicated for around 10 min. In order to passivate the surface of the samples, polyethylene glycol was utilized. The hydrothermal process temperature was optimized to be 180 °C for 3 h. Although, the hydrothermal process temperatures were varied from 110 to 220 °C for 90 min to 12 h duration. Following that, fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were collected and analyzed as shown in Fig. S1(a). The total intensity of FL emission was determined to be highest at 180 °C process temperature, as evidenced by the deconvolution results shown in Figs. S2–S3 and Table S1. Hence, the hydrothermal process temperature were optimized to be 180 °C for 3 h based on consistency and repeatability of FL emission.

After cooling to room temperature overnight, each as-prepared solution was centrifuged at 8000–9000 rpm for about 15 min. The contaminants settled after centrifugation, and the supernatant solution was collected. To remove any remaining contaminants not removed by centrifugation, the supernatant solution was filtered through a 220 nm syringe filter made of polytetrafluoroethylene membrane. Following that, the solution was freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C after filtration and stored for future use. The as-synthesized material's pH value was estimated to be around 4.17. The graphical representation of the complete procedure is depicted in Fig. 1 . However, the details of sample # 1 can be found from our previous publication [25]. The relative quantum yield in reference to quinine sulfate was determined to be about 18.2%.

The REMI R8C or CPR-30 Plus centrifuge machine was used to ultra-centrifugate the material. Using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer with a step of 1 nm in the wavelength bands of 230–700 nm and 400–750 nm, respectively, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were captured. Using a REMI MS-500 magnetic stirrer, the sample was stirred. Using a Shimadzu IR Spirit Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer in the wavelength region of 4000–400 cm−1, the type of surface functionalized CODs was evaluated. Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III measured the X-ray photoelectron spectra, which ranged from 0 to 1200 eV. Using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer at 40 kV, 40 mA, and a non-monochromatic Cu K
α X-ray with an angular range of 5-90° and an angular step of 0.02°, the crystalline character of the CQDs was ascertained. On a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer, measurements of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (13C NMR and 1H NMR) were made. The Anton Paar Litesizer 500 was used to measure the surface zeta potential. Using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) with a 200 KV accelerating voltage, the morphologies and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of CQDs were examined. The relative content of carbon and oxygen in as-synthesized CQDs was assessed using Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) on the JEOL JEM-2100F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several optical displays and LED prototypes were developed using environmentally sustainable CQDs integrated bioplastics. Biodegradable bioplastic was fabricated using readily accessible corn starch, vinegar, and glycerin. For this purpose, 200 mg of corn starch was added to a glycerin and vinegar (1:1 v/v) mixer and continuously stirred for 10–15 min on a hotplate maintained at 50 °C. Following that, different concentrations of either sample #1 or 2 (varying from 5 to 80 mg/ml) were added dropwise and mixed constantly for another 5–10 min. The CQD solution was well agitated in an ultrasonic bath for 5–10 min prior to mixing. Thin film of CQDs@bioplastic composites (containing samples #1 and 2) was then coated on a glass slide using a doctored blade process (see Fig. S4 for details).

Similar casting techniques were used on multiple CQDs solutions with pH values ranging from 2.54, 4.17, 7.4, 10.80. The CQDs@bioplastic composite films (containing samples #1 and 2) were then placed in either a prefabricated dome-shaped plastic mould encasing a commercial ultraviolet LED (UVC, 253.7 nm, 0.5 W) or a commercially available UVC-tube light (254 nm, 11 W) commonly used as a RO UV filter for water purification or germ sterilization and allowed to rest at room temperature for 24–48 h.

The morphological characteristics of intentionally P-doped CQDs (sample #2) were analyzed by HRTEM. Fig. 2 (a)-(b) displays the HRTEM images at different scales. The TEM micrographs demonstrate that the as-synthesized P-doped CQDs are quasi-circular or polygonal in shape, tiny, yet irregularly dispersed and agglomerated in some cases. Besides, lattice spacing (d-spacing) was determined through fast Fourier transformation, yielding a d-spacing value of 2.45 Å in relation to the graphite (002) lattice plane, as shown in Fig. 2(c) [26,27]. Furthermore, the corresponding histogram for the statistical distribution of CQDs is presented in Fig. 2(d), which demonstrates that the majority of CQD sizes are dispersed between 2 and 6 nm, with an average diameter of 3.4 nm, indicating that CQDs have a significant quantum confinement effect. The distributions clearly display characteristics of a Lorentzian distribution. This, we believe, is due to the quantum dots' roughly polygonal or circular form, the thickness of which is governed by the number of graphite layers. As a result, the diameter and thickness (number of graphite layers) distributions in the TEM size distributions are overlaid.

Furthermore, the broad diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns demonstrated the poor poly-crystalline structure of the deliberately P-doped CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(e). The elemental composition was determined by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), as shown in Fig. 2(f). EDX analysis reveals that CQDs are efficiently doped with P, as well as having high carbon (At. % 69.68) and oxygen (At. % 23.34) concentrations, as well as trace levels of nitrogen (At. % 3.92), calcium (At. % 1.42), and phosphorus (At. % 1.43). Moreover, the presence of oxygen is also evident arising due to the possibility of various functionalized groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl or carbonyl in the shell of CQDs. Therefore, EDX study reveals that as-synthesized CQDs is effectively doped with P, N and Ca (here denoted as P,N,Ca-doped CQDs). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also employed to investigate the bonding nature and compositions.

The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3 (a) confirms the existence of C, O, N, Ca, and P with binding energies of 284.6 eV, 531.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 343.5 eV, respectively. The atomic percentages obtained from the survey spectrum reveal that C, O, N, Ca, and P are present in approximately 64.67%, 29.38%, 2.8%, 1.63%, and 1.52%, respectively. These values are quite similar to those obtained from the EDX study. Again, the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3(b) reveals the presence of three different bonds with binding energies 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.7 eV arising from sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C–OH bonds [28–31]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra shown in Fig. 3(c) reveal just one peak, centered at 399.5 eV, indicating the existence of graphitic-N and demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were effectively doped in CQDs [30,31]. Similarly, the O1s spectra in Fig. 3(d) are determined to be composed of two components with centers at 530.6 eV and 531.9 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of the C= O and C–OH groups [28,29,31]. Accordingly, Ca 2p peak (Fig. 3(e)) was deconvoluted into two peaks centered about 343.5 eV and 347.4 eV, which display characteristic Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 peaks, respectively, confirming successful Ca doping in CQDs [32]. Again, the P 2p peak in Fig. 3(e) was deconvoluted into two peaks centered about 130.59 eV and 131.5 eV, corresponding to typical p
3/2 and p
1/2 peaks, confirming successful P doping in CQDs. Therefore, the XPS analysis once again reveals that phosphorus was effectively doped in Brassica flower extract derived CQDs in sample #2, thereby confirming the structure of N, Ca, P-doped CQDs.

The crystallinity of the as-synthesized N, Ca,P-doped CQDs was confirmed by XRD, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The obtained wide diffraction peak with a center angle of 23.8° corresponds to the (002) plane, which was attributed to the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs, resulting in a turbostratic structure (which is basically a hybridized carbon composed of graphitic, sp2-hybridized domains surrounded by an amorphous matrix) [32–35]. Furthermore, the amorphous nature of the produced particles contributed to the wide peak. Meanwhile, the appearance of few faint diffraction peaks at 42.2°, 43.9°, and 53.1° are appearing from the C (100), Ca (220), and P (242) planes, respectively (JCPDS Card No. 01-081-1161) [36].

FTIR measurements, on the other hand, revealed the existence of numerous functional groups and linkages either with intentionally or unintentionally doped CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Stretching frequencies of 3421.72, 2920.23, 2123.63, 1656.25, 1460.11,1099.43, and 939.33 cm−1, clearly suggest the presence of –OH, -C-H, C–N, CO, and –Ca, respectively [37]. The presence of Ca in the Brassica juncea flower extract is also shown. Usually, mustard extract includes a variety of phytochemicals, including vitamins, minerals (Ca, Mg, Zn etc.), dietary fiber, chlorophylls, glucosinolates (and their degradation products), polyphenols, and volatile components (allyl isothiocyanate, 3-butyl isothiocyanate, etc.). Therefore, using natural biomaterials is not only an economically and environmentally viable approach for green synthesis of CQDs, but the mineral content also aids in heteroatom doping in CQDs. However, the presence of -C-P was also evident at vibrational frequency of 457.87 cm−1. These hydrophilic functional groups enable the formation of highly water-soluble carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca, P-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since CQDs have outstanding optical characteristics, optical absorption was initially examined. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (a), the strong absorption peak for unintentionally doped CQDs (N,Ca-doped CQDs, sample #1) began around 517.76 nm with a trail continuing in the range up to 604.3 nm, whereas for intentionally P-doped CQDs (N,Ca,P-doped CQDs, sample #2) the maximum absorption peak was observed to be shifted to 464.34 nm along with trails shifted up to 595.7 nm.

This is presumably due to the typical π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band and the n–π* transitions in the graphitic core [38]. The presence of functional groups on the core's edge is believed to be responsible for the formation of the second absorption peak. This, in turn, will facilitate the transfer of negatively charged ions from the nonbonding orbital of the heteroatom to the core's π* orbital, leading in n–π* transitions in prepared CQDs. These absorption transitions between the core and edge states also generate hole-electron excitons. Furthermore, it has been observed that the threshold absorption peak is shifted towards higher wavelength (464.34 nm–517.76 nm) for N, Ca, P-doped CQDs as shown in Fig. 5(b). Meanwhile, the band gap was measured to vary from 2.88 eV following the conventional Tauc plot (see Fig. S4 for further details).

Carbon quantum dots, on the other hand, are often characterized by their emission wavelength and size-dependent fluorescence activity. Fig. 6 depicts the fluorescence spectra of both intentionally and unintentionally doped CQDs.

Following excitation at 280 nm, the FL spectra of intentionally P-doped CQDs (N, Ca, P-doped CQDs) were shown to release more intense emission than unintentionally doped CQDs, as shown in Fig. 6(a). The deconvolution of the FL spectra revealed three peaks, as shown in Fig. 6(b), with maximal emission peaks occurring at 421.78, 470.17 and 524.68 nm, respectively, with percentage contributions of 5.62%, 78.62%, and 15.74%, demonstrating the dominance of greenish-color fluorescence emission. The aqueous solutions of intentionally or unintentionally doped CQDs were pale yellow and clear in daylight, but when exposed to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), they fluorescence brightly and vividly green, as seen in Fig. 6 (c).
pH sensitive fluorescence is another unique property of CQDs. Hence, the influence of pH on fluorescence properties was also examined in this work.

The pH was adjusted to 2.54 by slowly adding aqueous hydrochloric acid (0.1 M HCl) solution, whereas the pH was increased to 10.80 by slowly adding aqueous sodium hydroxide (0.1 M NaOH). The pH-dependent FL spectra of N,Ca,P-doped CQDs is shown in Fig. 7 (a). It's worth noting that when the pH value rises from acid (pH:2.54) to base (pH:11), the emission peak expands due to more intense emission. Deconvolution of the obtained FL spectra given in Fig. 7(b)–(e) revealed that the largest contributing peak maxima shifts towards higher wavelength (458 nm for pH:2.54 vs. 469.6 nm for pH:11) when the pH value changes from acid to base. The observed variations in emission wavelength towards higher values are most likely caused by protonation or deprotonation of an oxygenated functional group.

Excitation dependent FL spectra, on the other hand, might be valuable in developing and implementing a down-conversion strategy for LEDs and UV tubes. Excitation-dependent FL emission was therefore investigated. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 250 to 535 nm, the emission wavelength increased from 359 to 583 nm, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). The color-contour map of photoemission is shown in Fig. 8(b), with the FL intensity represented by the color scale and the emission wavelength in the x-axis and the excitation in the y-axis, respectively. The fluorescence emission peaks shift towards redshift as the excitation wavelength rises, suggesting photon absorption. The findings demonstrate that intentionally P-doped CQDs have edge defects and excitation-dependent fluorescence, which are related to the quantum confinement effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Brassica juncea derived N,Ca,P-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence produced by CQDs varies with the excitation wavelength. Fluorescence color is frequently linked to electronic bandgap variations caused by conjugated π-domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and heteroatom doping. This study proposes a scheme for the structure of the energy bands on the basis of the XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectra of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca, P-doped CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 9 . The FL emission spectra that were observed to be excitation dependent, particularly on the lower energy side, as depicted in Fig. 8(a), clearly indicate the existence of a variety of chromophoric band-gap structures made by groups related to C, N, or Ca, such as C–C/CC (intrinsic-C), C–N (graphitic-N), C–Ca, CO groups, or grain boundaries [39]. In contrast, surface defects can serve as a capture site for excitons, resulting in surface defect state fluorescence. Surface defects are mostly caused by surface oxidation or dangling bonds. Under UV illuminations, the proposed optical band-gap structure of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca-doped CQDs and N, Ca, P-doped CQDs are depicted in Fig. 9(a) and (b), respectively. During the electron transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the carbon core, an electron-hole pair, or exciton, is produced when the localized π-electron in double bonds (mostly CC) absorbs UV photons.

This exciton may produce observed greenish color FL through radiative recombination following vibration relaxation via route 1. In contrast, after undergoing an inter-band transition from a higher LUMO to a lower LUMO that is longer than the absorbed wavelength, the excited electron may release further components of visible light through routes 2, 3, or 4. While intentional P doping may induce additional defects states that de-excite electrons following route 5, as seen in Fig. 9(b), this broadens the emission wavelength even further. Therefore, it is believed that intentional doping of P can further enhance the fluorescence compared to unintentionally N, Ca-doped CQDs, which could be useful in the design of down-conversion for LEDs and UV tubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Brassica juncea derived N,Ca,P-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite for down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes
ce:section.ce:para: Bioplastic is a more ecologically friendly and safer alternative to silicone resin and other commonly used synthetic polymers such as poly-N-vinyl carbazole (PVK), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), poly-methyl methacrylate (PMMA), and others. Bioplastics were chosen not only for their ease of use, low cost, and environmental friendliness, but also for their ability to avoid self-extinguishing in the solid form owing to agglomeration. The starch matrix, on the other hand, does not compete with the excitation light and does not absorb the CQD emission, assisting in the retention of fluorescence efficiency. In this regard, thin films were first fabricated after optimization using intentionally P-doped CQD@bioplastic composites, as shown in Fig. 10 . Further details of manufacturing stages taken during a trial of corn-starch-based bioplastic is shown in Fig. S3 in supplemental data.

The composite bioplastic film becomes green when exposed to UV radiation, whereas the bioplastic film does not. The intentionally P-doped composite film, on the other hand, emits a more vivid greenish color than the unintentionally doped composite film. Following that, N, Ca, P-doped CQDs@bioplastic were employed to fabricate down-conversion LEDs, the details of which are given in the experiment section.

Intentionally P-doped CQDs with different pH dependence (pH: 2.54 to 11.0), embedded in bioplastics at a specific concentration (5 mg/ml), were investigated for UVC LED down-conversion [40–45]. These pH-dependent down-conversion LEDs and their electroluminescence (EL) spectra are shown in Fig. 11 at a forward bias current of 15 mA. The appropriate chromatic coordinates were assessed in accordance with the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931) guidelines [45]. It can be noticed that brighter yellow-green colors are obtained for intentionally P-doped bioplastic composites compared to those without P. Color coordinates comparison of the two types of converted LEDs presented in Table S2 in supplementary data. The chromatic coordinates were found to change slightly from (0.37, 0.56) to (0.35, 0.58) as the pH value increased from acidic (pH:2.54) to base (pH:11).

In addition, a commercial UVC tube (254 nm, 11 W) commonly utilized for bacterial disinfection or RO water purifier has been chosen for large-scale applications of the produced ecologically sustainable bioplastic composite to expand its capabilities as a visible tube light beyond its normal usage. Fig. 12 (a) is a digital image of a commercial UVC tube that emits ultraviolet light. However, when the tube was merely wrapped with corn-starch bioplastic, no significant changes in emission color were observed, as shown in Fig. 12(b). Therefore, bioplastic film alone is transparent in ultraviolet region. Nonetheless, when the tube was wrapped in bioplastic with unintentionally doped CQDs, a milder greenish-yellow color was released, but an intense one was emitted for bioplastic embedded with intentionally P-doped CQDs, as seen in Fig. 12 (c) and (d), respectively. The corresponding electroluminescence spectra, as well as CIE chromatic coordinates, demonstrate this.

This example clearly demonstrates how phosphorus doped in N, Ca-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite may increase the down-conversion efficiency of UV LEDs and tubes. However, future research might focus on the fundamental optical features (such as CIE and efficiency) of CQDs@bioplastic composite.

In summary, an economical and ecologically sustainable green synthesis process for developing carbon quantum dots embedded in bioplastic composite is reported. CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract with or without phosphorus addition have been investigated. CQDs were unintentionally doped with nitrogen and calcium without the inclusion of any doping element precursors. Phosphorus, on the other hand, was intentionally doped in order to improve the luminous characteristics of CQDs. HRTEM, EDX, XPS, XRD, FTIR, NMR, and other techniques were used to investigate the morphological, structural, and physicochemical properties of P-doped CQDs. With an average diameter of 3.4 nm, the quasi-circular or polygonal-shaped CQDs were revealed to exhibit yellow-greenish fluorescence following UV stimulation, to have outstanding photostability, and to be extremely water soluble, with a quantum yield of up to 18.2%. The photoluminescence characteristics, as well as possible mechanisms, were also examined, with intentionally P-doped CQDs demonstrating superior luminous properties than unintentionally doped CQDs. On the other hand, due to the severe environmental repercussions of synthetic plastics, the development of biodegradable polymers for industrial and commercial usage is critical today. Thus, as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs, the intentionally P-doped embedded in corn-starch-based bioplastic thin film was fabricated to demonstrate down-conversion of LEDs and UV tubes. Furthermore, the effects of pH sensitivity on the down-conversion efficiency of bioplastic composites in the application of LEDs and UV tubes have been investigated. The Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931) chromatic coordinates were compared. This experiment clearly demonstrates how phosphorus-doped N,Ca-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite may improve the efficiency of UV LEDs and tubes. However, future research might focus on the fundamental optical characteristics (such as CIE and luminous efficiency) of CQDs@bioplastic composite.

Varun Dutt Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Vishal Kansay: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anita Bhatia: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2023.127834.
